g.data-narration: Welcome to System Calls - the essential bridge between user programs and the operating system kernel.
g.text: The Interface Between Programs and OS Kernel

g.data-narration: A system call is a programmatic interface where user programs request services from the operating system kernel.
g.text: A system call is the programmatic interface through which a user-level program
requests a service from the OS kernel. It provides a controlled gateway to
kernel mode (privileged access) where the OS performs the requested operation.

g.data-narration: User programs run in restricted user mode and cannot directly access hardware or perform privileged operations safely.
The system call process involves ten key steps from the user program call to returning the result.
The CPU switches from user mode to kernel mode when a TRAP instruction (software interrupt) is executed.
g.text: Mode Switch: User Mode ↔ Kernel Mode
Limited access • Cannot execute privileged instructions
Full hardware access • All instructions allowed

g.data-narration: Understanding the three-layer hierarchy: API provides portable functions, system call interface maps them to kernel handlers.
g.text: API vs System Call vs System Call Interface

g.data-narration: Three major APIs exist: POSIX for Unix/Linux, Win32 for Windows, and Java API for cross-platform applications.
Method 1: Parameters placed in CPU registers - fastest but limited by number of available registers.
Method 2: Parameters stored in a memory block; address passed via register - unlimited parameters, used by Linux and Solaris.
g.text: Parameter Passing: Method 2 - Block/Table in Memory

g.data-narration: Method 3: Parameters pushed onto stack by program, popped by OS - unlimited parameters but slower.
Comparing all three methods: registers (fastest), block/table (unlimited, used by Linux), and stack (unlimited).
Tracing the read() system call: from user program call through C library, mode switch, kernel handler, back to user.
g.text: Example: read() System Call Detailed Flow

g.data-narration: Each system call has a unique number. The kernel maintains a table mapping these numbers to handler functions.
System calls are the interface between user programs and kernel, invoked via TRAP, causing mode switch with three parameter passing methods.
g.text: 1. Interface between user programs and OS kernel
2. Invoked via TRAP/INT instruction - causes mode switch (User ↔ Kernel)
3. Parameters passed via: Registers (fast), Block/Table (Linux), or Stack
4. API (POSIX, Win32) wraps system calls for portability
